revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:粟嶋神社 (宇土市)]]
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Awashima Shrine |image = File:Awashima torii.jpg |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |location = Shinkai-machi 557, Uto City, Kumamoto Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|32.69969444444445|N|130.63366666666667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = [[:en:Sukunahikona no Mikoto|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]] |type = Former Unranked Shrines (unranked shrine) |founded = Kan'ei 10 (1633) |honden = |other_names = |pilgrimage_sites = |festival = March 3 |rituals = |map = {{location map|Japan Kumamoto Prefecture|label=Awashima Shrine|position=top|width=256|marksize=8|relief=1|qq=}} |location = {{ill|Uto|qid=Q861262|1=Uto, Kumamoto}} }} '''Awashima Shrine''' (粟嶋神社, Awashima Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Uto City]], {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, Japan. == deity == The shrine enshrines '''[[:en:Sukunahikona no Mikoto|Sukunahikona no Mikoto]]''', also revered as '''Awashima Daimyōjin'''. == History == According to legend, in '''Kan'ei 10 (1633)''', an elderly couple residing in the area provided lodging for a traveling Buddhist monk. That night, the wife had a dream in which '''Awashima Daimyōjin''' appeared to her. The following morning, after the monk had departed, the couple discovered a divine statue left behind in the room. Believing it to be an incarnation of Awashima Daimyōjin, they enshrined it, marking the shrine’s origin. Later, in '''Bunka 12 (1815)''', a shrine building was constructed, which was later rebuilt in '''Shōwa 24 (1949)'''. == Festivals == The main annual '''festival''' (例祭) is held on '''March 3'''. == Other Features == * '''Mini Torii (Koshinobe Torii)''' - Within the shrine grounds, there is a '''30 cm-square torii''' dating back to '''Bunka 11 (1814)'''. It is believed that passing through this small torii can help cure various ailments, especially those related to women’s health. During the annual festival, many worshippers visit the shrine to pass through this torii, praying for good health. == Access == === By Train === * {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|qid=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Misumi Line|en|Misumi Line|qid=Q871157|lt=Misumi Line|1=Misumi Line}}: About a '''10-minute walk''' from '''{{ill|Midorikawa Station|en|Midorikawa Station|qid=Q6842946|lt=Midorikawa Station|1=Midorikawa Station}}'''. === By Car === * {{ill|Kyushu Expressway|en|Kyushu Expressway|qid=Q1060803|lt=Kyushu Expressway|1=Kyushu Expressway}}: About '''20 minutes by car''' from '''[[Matsuhashi Interchange]]'''. Parking available. == Related Literature == * [[Takashi Suzuki (historian)|Takashi Suzuki]], *Kumamoto no Jinja to Jiin* (Shrines and Temples of Kumamoto), [[Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun]], 1980, pp. 86–87. * *Kumamoto Prefectural Encyclopedia*, edited and published by [[Kumamoto Nichinichi Shimbun]], 1982, p. 41. * *Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 44: Kumamoto-ken no Chimei* (Historical Place Names of Kumamoto Prefecture), {{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|qid=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha|1=Heibonsha}}, 1985. * *Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 43: Kumamoto-ken* (Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary: Kumamoto Prefecture), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|en|Kadokawa Shoten|qid=Q1054765|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|1=Kadokawa Shoten}}, 1987, p. 118. == External Links == *[http://www.awashima.or.jp/ 粟嶋神社]（神社公式） {{神道 横|qq=}} {{shinto-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|粟嶋神社 (宇土市)|version=85195890|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:粟嶋神社 (宇土市)]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
